roots.title: Advanced SQL Concepts and Data Management Techniques
roots.summary: The segment covers advanced SQL topics including grouping, ordering, and filtering, emphasizing SQL readability and the use of the SELECT keyword for data retrieval from the 'dragon' table in the docDB database. It details query execution, result display, and the use of ORDER BY, LIMIT, OFFSET, and sampling techniques. It clarifies that row order is non-deterministic unless specified and explains sorting with ORDER BY, including handling ties and using ASC for ascending order. The importance of a seed value for consistent pseudo-random sequences in sampling is highlighted, ensuring reproducibility. Efficient SQL sampling methods are discussed alongside the GROUP BY clause for analyzing categories in a dish database, calculating averages, sums, and comparing prices. The segment also addresses type conversion using the CAST function and the use of the CASE statement for conditional logic to create new columns based on specified conditions.

children.title: Advanced SQL Concepts: Query Syntax and Data Management
children.summary: The segment introduces advanced SQL topics such as grouping, ordering, and filtering, with a focus on SQL's readability. It emphasizes the SELECT keyword for data retrieval from the 'dragon' table in the docDB database, detailing query execution, result display, and the use of ORDER BY, LIMIT, OFFSET, and sampling techniques. It explains that row order in database queries is non-deterministic unless specified, and discusses sorting data using ORDER BY, including handling ties and using ASC for ascending order. The LIMIT and OFFSET keywords are explained for restricting output, along with an introduction to the JupyterLab interface for SQL queries, highlighting the importance of understanding SQL structure including SELECT, FROM, and WHERE clauses.

children.title: Advanced SQL Concepts and Query Syntax
children.summary: The lecture introduces advanced SQL topics including grouping, ordering, and filtering, paralleling concepts from Pandas. The instructor emphasizes SQL's readability and reassures students about understanding the material. A demonstration of SQL code will follow, revisiting basic queries before advancing to complex topics. The segment covers SQL query syntax with a focus on the SELECT keyword for data retrieval from the 'dragon' table in the docDB database, detailing how to execute queries, display results relationally, and use ORDER BY, LIMIT, OFFSET, and sampling techniques.

children.title: Advanced SQL Concepts: Grouping, Ordering, Filtering
children.summary: The lecture introduces advanced SQL topics, focusing on group by, ordering of data, and filtering, paralleling concepts previously covered in Pandas. The instructor emphasizes the readability of SQL, likening it to English text, and reassures students that they don't need to understand every detail but should follow along with the explanations. The session will include a demonstration of SQL code, revisiting basic queries from the last lecture before moving into more complex topics.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Let me set this. Okay, wonderful. This is our next SQL lecture. So things are going to be more interesting today in a sense that what I'll do is I'll go into more advanced SQL stuff. So kind of think about this as the previous lecture as the first two lectures of Pandas that I was translating it into SQL and then today is actually the second two lectures of Pandas. So I'm going to talk about how we can actually do group by, how we can do ordering of the data in a table or relation, and how can we actually do filtering. I remember filter function that you had in DataFrame. So we're going to see that at the equivalent of that in SQL as well. And then there's going to be a lot of demo. So you're going to see a lot of SQL code. You don't need to understand everything, but the important pieces that you just be able to follow what I'm explaining to you. Again, SQL is really interesting that you can actually read it as you're reading in English text. So every complicated even SQL query that I'll show you, you can read it again and it will make sense to you. So I'm going to finish with the basic queries that we had from last time. And I'm going to actually jump into the demo to just quickly remind you of what we started seeing last time. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X

children.title: SQL Query Syntax and Data Retrieval Techniques
children.summary: The segment discusses SQL query syntax, highlighting the use of the SELECT keyword to retrieve specific columns or all columns from a table. It references the 'dragon' table and the connection to the docDB database. The lecture also covers executing queries, displaying results in a relational format, and introduces concepts like ordering data with ORDER BY, restricting output using LIMIT and OFFSET, and sampling data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I'm still in the lecture 20 demo. All of the kind of thick hood is actually ported to also lecture 21. So just feel free to open the notebook for lecture 21 and take a look at that. So what we've seen in the previous lecture, if I can find it, is that we had seen a bunch of different syntax. So we had seen that we can use select keyword, and then we pass a number of columns that we want to select, or we can say star, meaning that give me all the columns that you have and all the rows that you have. And then I'm saying from the table dragon. You have seen before that how I can actually connect to the database and actually make sure that the docDB database is actually there to run my query, but let's take a look at how it looks like. So it says that it's running the query, it ran the query on the docDB database. So that's the alias that I picked for, the docDB connection that I made to the docDB file or the database file that I had. And this is the drag on data frame, sorry, not data frame anymore. This is a relation. So what I have is a bunch of different attributes, a bunch of different columns exactly in the same table or format that you're expecting from a table and also similar to what you had in data frame. children.title: Non-deterministic Row Order in Databases
children.summary: The order of rows returned from a database query is non-deterministic unless explicitly specified. This variability occurs due to database optimization in data retrieval. To enforce a specific order, such as by year, the appropriate SQL syntax must be used.

children.title: Non-deterministic Row Order in Databases
children.summary: The order of rows returned from a database query is non-deterministic unless specified otherwise. This means that the order may vary based on how the database optimizes data retrieval. If a specific order is desired, such as ordering by year, it can be enforced using the appropriate SQL syntax.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Remember that I could also say that, oh, I don't want everything in your table. I can just get name and year and just give me name in year and I'll give you the name and year. What you're going to see today is that the order of the rows that you're going to get is not going to be deterministic. That's an important thing to note from databases. In fact, when I was playing with the demo, I kind of confused myself at one point that, oh, why is the order? different and why is it not the order that I'm seeing in the screen shots that you have in the lecture. And this is exactly because the order is actually going to be not random, but it's just going to be non-deterministic. The order that the rows are going to be returned to you is actually going to be the order that the database can optimize. So however, it can get you the data that's the fastest way, it will give you the data. Unless you specify that you want a certain ordering. If you specify that you want a certain ordering, like you want to order by year or whatever, it will make sure that it gives you the data in the same order that you specified. So if I want to go ahead and say that, okay, I want name and year, but now I don't want you to decide about the ordering of the rows, but I want the order to to be, for example, ordered by year. So let's sort the data frame or this relation by year. So the syntax that we'll see is Actually, I'll go ahead and say... Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab >

leaf_chunk.audio: Remember that I could also say that, oh, I don't want everything in your table. I can just get name and year and just give me name in year and I'll give you the name and year. What you're going to see today is that the order of the rows that you're going to get is not going to be deterministic. That's an important thing to note from databases. In fact, when I was playing with the demo, I kind of confused myself at one point that, oh, why is the order? different and why is it not the order that I'm seeing in the screen shots that you have in the lecture. And this is exactly because the order is actually going to be not random, but it's just going to be non-deterministic. The order that the rows are going to be returned to you is actually going to be the order that the database can optimize. So however, it can get you the data that's the fastest way, it will give you the data. Unless you specify that you want a certain ordering. If you specify that you want a certain ordering, like you want to order by year or whatever, it will make sure that it gives you the data in the same order that you specified. So if I want to go ahead and say that, okay, I want name and year, but now I don't want you to decide about the ordering of the rows, but I want the order to to be, for example, ordered by year. children.title: Sorting and Limiting Data in SQL
children.summary: The segment covers sorting data in SQL using the ORDER BY clause, detailing how to order results by year in both ascending and descending order, and how to handle ties by ordering alphabetically. It emphasizes the use of ASC for ascending order. The discussion includes the LIMIT and OFFSET keywords to restrict output, explaining how LIMIT specifies the number of rows returned and OFFSET skips a specified number of rows. Additionally, it introduces the JupyterLab interface for SQL queries and highlights the importance of understanding SQL query structure, including the SELECT and FROM keywords, and the optional WHERE clause for filtering data.

children.title: Sorting Data in SQL with ORDER BY
children.summary: The segment discusses how to sort data in SQL using the ORDER BY clause. It explains how to order results by year in ascending order and how to specify descending order if needed. Additionally, it covers handling ties by ordering alphabetically by name when years are the same, demonstrating the concept with examples. The importance of using ASC for ascending order is also highlighted.

children.title: Ordering Data and Using LIMIT/OFFSET in SQL
children.summary: Data can be ordered in SQL using ascending (ASC) or descending (DESC) specifications for columns. The lecture discusses how to specify which columns should be sorted in each order and introduces the concepts of LIMIT and OFFSET for restricting output. A matching question is posed to help students understand the application of LIMIT and OFFSET.

children.title: Understanding SQL Limit and Offset Functions
children.summary: The segment explains the SQL commands 'LIMIT' and 'OFFSET'. 'LIMIT' restricts the number of rows returned by a query, with 'LIMIT 2' returning the first two rows of a dataset. 'OFFSET' allows skipping a specified number of rows; for example, 'LIMIT 2 OFFSET 1' retrieves the second and third rows. The discussion emphasizes the importance of understanding the structure of SQL queries, particularly the 'SELECT' command, which specifies the columns to retrieve, using '*' for all columns or listing specific ones.

What offset does is that offset says that, okay, give me some more of the rows, but then offset it by a factor or by a number that I don't want the first one, for example, and I want the second one and the third one. So that's what offset does. So when we specify that there is the limit of two, it will get the first two. When we specify that limit two but offset by one, that means that get tree, discard the first one and then give me the second one and the third one which in this case is going to give us these two rows, okay? children.title: SQL Query Structure and Filtering Techniques
children.summary: SQL queries require the SELECT and FROM keywords, with additional keywords like WHERE, ORDER BY, LIMIT, and OFFSET being optional. The WHERE clause is used for filtering data, such as selecting rows where cuteness is greater than zero. LIMIT specifies the number of rows to return, while OFFSET indicates how many initial rows to skip. For better readability, it is recommended to write each SQL keyword on a separate line and to use all caps for keywords.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Sometimes it would also do operations on the columns. Remember that I was doing I think, uh, cuteness times two. So we could do that too. I would say that I want cuteness, but I want it to be multiplied by two, um, as well. Uh, we need to always specify the table that we're going to extract our data from. Uh, we also used their clause with a predicate in order to do filtering. Remember that I was saying that where cuteness is greater than zero in order to extract all of the cuter dragons. Um, so that's the kind of filtering that we can do. Now we have also seen limit and offset. Limit is going to tell you how many rows you want, and then offset is going to tell you how many of the initial rows it should ignore and then give you everything else. Know that what we also talked about before is kind of the standard for writing SQL queries that each one of these keywords are going to be in a separate line so that the code is a little bit more readable. children.title: SQL Best Practices and Sampling Techniques
children.summary: SQL keywords should be written in all caps, although the version used is not case-sensitive. It's best practice to end statements with a semicolon, even though errors may not occur without it. The discussion also introduces the concept of sampling in data frames, highlighting the importance of this technique as previously practiced in homework. Two methods of sampling are mentioned, starting with shuffling all rows of an entire table.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Also, all of the keywords are going to be in all caps. The version of SQL that we're using is not going to be very sensitive to that. So you can just type the lower case, but it's good to get used to that practice generally. It always ends with a semicolon. Again, the version of SQL that we're using is not going to give you errors, so you can actually not include semicolon. In fact, I tried different versions of this in the previous lecture, but I didn't put semicolon or I used, for example, title case for keywords and it was not thrown in error. but know that this is the best practice is just to go with this structure. So let's jump back to the demo one more time. There's the next concept of sampling. So we've done sampling in data frame. We know that we need sampling. You have done a lot of sampling and bootstrap analysis in previous homework. So we need to do that and learn how we can do that in data frames as in. relations as well. There are two ways that we can do it. So there is one way that you would say that go ahead, grab the entire table, shuffle all the rows. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X

children.title: Random Sampling Techniques in SQL
children.summary: Random sampling from large datasets can be resource-intensive if done by shuffling the entire dataset. Instead, reservoir sampling is introduced as an efficient method to randomly select a specified number of rows without needing to randomize the entire database. Examples of SQL commands are provided: one using 'ORDER BY RANDOM()' to limit results to two rows, and another using 'SAMPLE reservoir(2 ROWS) REPEATABLE (100)' for more efficient sampling.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: and just give me for example the top 10 or the top 20 or whatever right. There is the issue with that approach that if you have a very large data set it's going to be very resource intensive that you really shuffle the whole thing like gigabytes and terabytes of data. So that's not really ideal. The way to do that is actually this code that you're seeing here that we would say that order by random or the by random is going to actually randomize the entire table. and then it has a final table and then you're saying that limit to just give me two so it's a lot of work for two rows that you want to sample instead what we would do is we would use something that's called reservoir sampling so reservoir sampling says that I'm just going to just be able to go ahead and grab the number of rows that you want from your entire set or your entire data set randomly. You don't need to randomize it and babysit me. So in this sense, here sample reservoir is a function that is going to actually do that sampling without shuffling the entire data, the entire database. children.title: Importance of Seed in Random Sampling
children.summary: Setting a seed value is essential for generating consistent pseudo-random sequences in computational tasks, ensuring reproducibility in data sampling. The same seed yields the same random rows on each execution, which is vital for demonstrating predictable results. Different seed values create different sequences, allowing for controlled randomization while still enabling result replication.

children.title: Importance of Seed in Random Sampling
children.summary: Setting a seed value is essential for generating consistent pseudo-random sequences in computational tasks. It ensures reproducibility in data sampling, meaning the same seed will yield the same random rows on each execution, which is vital for demonstrating predictable results. Different seed values create different sequences, allowing for controlled randomization while still enabling result replication.

children.title: Using Seed for Random Sampling in Notebooks
children.summary: Setting a seed value is crucial for generating pseudo-random sequences in computational tasks. When a seed is established, it ensures that the sequence of random values generated is consistent and deterministic, allowing for reproducibility in data sampling. This means that using the same seed will yield the same set of random rows every time the code is executed, which is important for demonstrating results in a predictable manner. Different seed values will produce different sequences, enabling controlled randomization while maintaining the ability to replicate results.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What we are also specifying here is that I want two rows. So sample two rows however you want to do it and then give that to me. One other important kind of thing that I included here, which is also going to be generally helpful when you're going to write a notebook that you can pass it to someone else is that we set a seat. You might have seen seed setting before in some of the notebooks. You're going to learn more about it in the capabilities courses but essentially what's happening is that every time that we're randomizing something on a computer we're not like 100% randomizing things it's the pseudo-random and the pseudo random means that we're just basically going to use a seed value and this seed value is going to generate a random set of following values that be one so. That set once you set the seed then the entire sequence that you're sampling from that seed is going to be constant and predictable and deterministic. So the only thing that is going to randomize the process is that if you use a different seed number then the sequence is going to be different. So a lot of time we want to actually use this property in order to one randomize the data that we're collecting or the data that we're sampling. But we want to be able to extract the same random every time that we run the notebook. I don't want to randomly select two separate rows every time. Specifically because when I run this code, I want to be able to deterministically tell you what is the set of two random rows that I'll be able to get so that I can put it on the slides. Reproducibility is really important, but then also there is a notion that we always want to randomize the stuff for us. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab

children.title: Efficient SQL Sampling, GROUP BY, and CASE Statements
children.summary: The segment discusses efficient SQL sampling methods, emphasizing the use of a seed for reproducibility, and revisits the GROUP BY clause for analyzing categories in a dish database, calculating average costs, and comparing prices across dish types. It covers calculating average, sum, minimum, and maximum values, and the importance of aliasing for clearer column names. Type conversion in SQL is also addressed, focusing on converting data types using the CAST function, with examples of renaming columns for clarity. The CASE statement is explained for implementing conditional logic, allowing the creation of new columns based on specified conditions. Lastly, a SQL query is demonstrated to classify movies into categories based on their start year.

children.title: Efficient SQL Sampling and GROUP BY Analysis
children.summary: The segment covers efficient sampling methods in SQL, emphasizing the use of a seed for reproducibility. It revisits grouping data, particularly using the GROUP BY clause to analyze categories within a dish database, such as appetizers and desserts. The lecture explains how to use SELECT with GROUP BY to extract unique categories and calculate average costs, comparing prices across dish types. It also discusses calculating average, sum, minimum, and maximum values for different dish types and highlights the importance of aliasing for clearer column names in query results. Lastly, the order of operations in SQL queries is introduced.

children.title: Efficient Sampling and SQL Grouping Concepts
children.summary: The segment discusses efficient sampling methods in SQL, highlighting the use of a seed for reproducibility. It then revisits the concept of grouping data, emphasizing its importance in analyzing different categories within a dataset. An example is provided using a dish database, illustrating how to use the GROUP BY clause in SQL to study various types of dishes, such as appetizers and desserts, independently.

children.title: Using GROUP BY to Analyze Dish Categories
children.summary: The lecture discusses how to use the SELECT statement with the GROUP BY clause to categorize data from the dish table. It explains that grouping by 'type' allows for the extraction of unique categories such as entree, dessert, and appetizer. Additionally, the average cost of dishes within each category can be calculated to compare prices across different types. The importance of using GROUP BY not just for distinct values but also for obtaining information from those groups is emphasized.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is what you have already seen. This is the relation that we're going to be working with. One thing that I want to show you is that, okay, if I go ahead and select the type from this relation, what's that going to look like? So I'm going to say that this is select type. of from So go ahead and copy this over so that things are easier and you can follow the structure, I would say that select type. from the dish table, but then go ahead and group by the column that I want. Group by type. Right? Praying is neat to stuff. And now I have the grouped categories. Right? So this is kind of a way that you can get the unique, we had distinct keyword that would give you the distinct set of values that you have, but this is also another way that I'm getting the distinct set of values in the color. But remember that in GroupBuy, we were not concerned with just using GroupBuy in order to get distinct values, but we wanted to actually get some information from those groups, labeled by each group category. My group categories here are entree, dessert, and appetizer. But I want to, for example, say that if I go ahead and I take a look, I want to actually calculate average of the cost of all of those dishes in each one of the category, so that I can compare that on average, how much more expensive an entree dish is compared to an appetizer. children.title: Calculating Average and Other Stats in SQL
children.summary: The segment discusses how to calculate the average cost of dishes using SQL with a GROUP BY clause. It demonstrates querying the average, sum, minimum, and maximum values for different dish types, specifically showing results for entrees, desserts, and appetizers. The SQL commands used include SELECT with AVG, SUM, MIN, and MAX functions applied to the 'cost' and 'name' columns from the 'Dish' table.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I want to get the average of the cost. Right, this is kind of group by act. So this is just doing act thing on top of the group by. So, but I'm specifying what columns, what column I want exactly. So now I have average of the costs, Entrez 10, Desert 5, and Apatizer. Okay. Very interesting. I could also actually go ahead and say I want a bunch of different stats about each one of the categories not only that you tell me different categories of type that I have but give me the sum of the cost the minimum of the cost and also maximum of name for some reason I'm interested in max. children.title: Aggregation Functions and Aliasing in SQL
children.summary: The segment discusses how to group data by specific columns and apply aggregation functions such as AVG, SUM, MIN, and MAX in SQL. It emphasizes the importance of aliasing to create cleaner and more meaningful column names in query results, demonstrating how to rename aggregated columns for clarity. The use of aggregation functions is highlighted as essential for extracting valuable insights from grouped data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And if we go ahead and run this, you would be able to see that each one of those columns are going to be added. Right? So let's go back exactly what's happening is that we... Grab a column that we want to group rose by. What's happening is that it groups rose by all of those categories, and then it will have one representative category, pair each group. And then what we can do on top of that is we can use some aggregation functions that I use one of them and you saw I used AVG as an average. You also saw that we took the max of the name. We took for example some of the cost. you can get first, you can get last, you can get minimum. So all of the aggregation functions that you've seen before is also available here so you can keep adding columns and just get more and more information. One thing to note here is that when I did some of the cost, this is also naming that as some of the cost, which is not going to be very exciting, but you remember that last time what we had was that, we said we can use aliasing so you don't need to actually use the name that you're not happy with, but you can say that I'm going to just select type and select average of cost. And I'm going to call this average of cost as average cost. and i'm gonna run it and now it's average cost How are we doing? Good. Not good. Um, so, Ellie, if thing is going to be used a lot with all of those aggregation functions, because then the column names are going to be really, uh, not clean and we might not really be interested in those. children.title: Order of Operations in SQL Queries
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the order of operations in SQL queries, emphasizing why certain tasks are not explicitly specified in the query. It highlights the importance of the GROUP BY clause in determining how data is aggregated, using the example of summing costs grouped by type from a 'Dish' table.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Uh, there is the other, uh, oh, uh, this is also interesting that I wanted to tell you. I think I briefly mentioned last time about the order of operation that's happening in the SQL query and why we go by a certain order and why we're not specifying, uh, how a certain tasks should be done. children.title: Using SQL for Grouping and Summarization
children.summary: In SQL, the process of grouping data is straightforward, as it allows users to specify what they want without detailing the steps to achieve it. When using the 'GROUP BY' clause, you indicate the type of data to aggregate, such as summing costs, without initially explaining the calculation method. SQL is a declarative language, making it easier to express intentions compared to procedural languages like Python. Users should be aware of the fixed order of operations in SQL: SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, and ORDER BY. This order will be provided in the reference sheet for the final exam.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: For example, when we were doing group by in pandas or in Python, what we had to do was specifically walk Python through the process that go ahead and group by and once you did that, there's an ag and there is this kind of function and then this is how you can summarize it. But notice here, the thing that we're doing is that we're just specifically saying what we're interested in. We're saying that give me type, give me some of of the cost. We're not saying how to go and calculate some of the cost until very later in the query, where we're saying that once you group by type then you can go ahead and some of the cost, right? So one nice thing about SQL is that it's a declarative programming language, language, where you just specify what you want. You don't walk the programming language true how to get there, which is very kind of easy and interesting to use. You might be tempted to actually walk SQL through doing this stuff and then you might kind of forget the order of different operations or different synth or keyboards that you have to use. It will take time to get used to how SQL can actually run queries and it's actually very clover in, figuring out what you're intending. So you might find yourself. kind of mixing between like your Python kind of way of thinking versus the sequel way of thinking and stuff. So again, we just go by a fixed order of operation. A fixed order of operation that you have seen so far is that we do select, we do from, we say which table is from, we do if we're going to do any filtering or not, we do group by, and then we do order by. Okay, this is the order and in the reference sheet for the final exam, you will get the order of the things or the keywords that you should use so you should be able to follow exactly the same thing. children.title: Aggregation Functions in Group-By Operations
children.summary: The segment discusses the use of aggregation functions in group-by operations, specifically focusing on size and count in data frames. Size indicates the total number of elements in a data frame or group-by object, while count measures the number of non-null values. The example illustrates grouping data by year and counting non-null values, highlighting that count will reflect the actual number of valid entries, while size counts all entries including nulls. The segment emphasizes the translation of these concepts from data frames to SQL.

children.title: SQL Column Expressions and Aggregation Functions
children.summary: Column expressions in SQL allow for direct calculations or the use of aggregation functions on selected columns. The syntax for SQL statements includes keywords like SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, LIMIT, and OFFSET, which should be written in all caps for clarity. Additionally, the AS keyword can be used to rename columns during the selection process, and aggregation functions such as MAX and MIN can be applied to column expressions.

children.title: Using HAVING for Group Filtering in SQL
children.summary: The filter function in data frames allows for grouping and determining which groups appear in the output based on a Boolean condition. If the condition is true, all rows from that group are included; if false, none are. The HAVING clause is introduced as a more intuitive method for filtering groups after applying conditions. It enables selection of groups based on specified criteria, determining whether each group should appear in the output.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is AG, or this is like AVG on the cost to give me the average of the cost in each one of the categories. So now into the business of filtering groups. So filter function when we had it in um, data frame was really interesting and was very unique. So filter function was telling you that, okay, you group by a certain thing. And I'm going to decide which one of those groups is going to shop in the output. So the entire data from group by object was either going to shop in the the output was going to be discarded. And the condition that we have been using was a certain Boolean condition that we would pass the entire data from group by object and we would check a condition and then we would decide whether the output is true or false. If the output was true, the entire data from group by object all the rows in that category would be moved to the output otherwise none of them would be moved to the output. So that... Syntax that we have here for filtering it or the keyboard is called having. So with having we would say that I'm going to select columns from the table and I'm grouping by a certain column that I have. and give me all of the do this processing and give me all of the groups that have a certain condition. So it makes a little bit more sense compared to the filter syntax. So here we would say that having all of this condition applied to each one of the groups, either that group is going to show up or that group is not going to show up in the output. Video: 1: lec20-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X
Lec 21 - DS100 Fa24 - SQL II - C X

I
EDA in SQL
Our typical workflow when working with "big data" is:
· Use SQL to query data from a database
· Use Python (with pandas ) to analyze this data in detail
We can, however, still perform simple data cleaning and re-structuring using SQL directly. To do so, we'll consider the Title table from the IMDB
dataset.
Simple
1
$.

leaf_chunk.audio: This is AG, or this is like AVG on the cost to give me the average of the cost in each one of the categories. So now into the business of filtering groups. So filter function when we had it in um, data frame was really interesting and was very unique. So filter function was telling you that, okay, you group by a certain thing. And I'm going to decide which one of those groups is going to shop in the output. So the entire data from group by object was either going to shop in the the output was going to be discarded. And the condition that we have been using was a certain Boolean condition that we would pass the entire data from group by object and we would check a condition and then we would decide whether the output is true or false. If the output was true, the entire data from group by object all the rows in that category would be moved to the output otherwise none of them would be moved to the output. So that... Syntax that we have here for filtering it or the keyboard is called having. So with having we would say that I'm going to select columns from the table and I'm grouping by a certain column that I have. and give me all of the do this processing and give me all of the groups that have a certain condition. So it makes a little bit more sense compared to the filter syntax. So here we would say that having all of this condition applied to each one of the groups, either that group is going to show up or that group is not going to show up in the output.

children.title: Counting Categories with SQL Group By
children.summary: The lecture discusses how to use SQL to group data by type and count the number of rows in each category from the 'Dish' relation. It emphasizes filtering categories where the maximum cost is less than 8. The SQL command 'GROUP BY type HAVING MAX(cost) < 8' is demonstrated, showing how to retrieve counts for entrees, appetizers, and desserts under this condition.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So let me switch. 2, uh... Yes, here we are. So here I have this entire, I'm working with the dish data frame. Not data frame again. This is relation. So with a bunch of different columns and here I would say that go ahead and grab the type. Right? And go ahead and count the number of rows that I have in each one of the categories. And where do those categories come from? The categories are coming from the type. Still we're grouping by by the type. And here I'm saying that I want to make sure that I only consider categories that have the max of the cost be less than 8. if I go ahead and just take a look at what's happening here and color coded again. So I have all of these entrees. I have all of these appetizers and I have this dessert right. So that's my group by that's the result. What we are interested in is actually we're getting the type and we're getting the count. So the type is going to show up. So the type is going to be an untray appetizer or dessert, but it's going to show up only if I am meeting this condition for a group. Maximum of the cost is less than eight. What is the maximum of the cost here? max of the cost for this collection is going to be 10. Video: 1: lec21-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X

children.title: SQL Query for Dish Types and Counts
children.summary: The discussion focuses on an SQL query that groups dishes by type and counts them, specifically filtering those with a maximum cost of less than 8. It explains that only the categories meeting the HAVING condition will be returned, leading to an interest in the types of dishes, such as appetizers and desserts. The results are noted to be returned without a specific order unless an ORDER BY clause is included, highlighting the importance of explicitly specifying the desired order in SQL queries.

children.title: Filtering with HAVING vs. WHERE in SQL
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the difference between using the HAVING clause and the WHERE clause in SQL for filtering data. The WHERE clause filters rows before grouping, while HAVING filters after grouping. An example is provided where rows with a cost less than 8 are excluded using the WHERE clause, followed by a GROUP BY operation to categorize the remaining data. The results indicate the number of remaining rows per type, emphasizing the distinction between row filtering and group filtering.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Um... So, comparison with Ver, because Ver also does filtering. So where was doing filtering on the rows? Having is doing filtering on the categories. So when I'm doing filtering on the rows using ver, here I would basically cross out all of the rows with the cost being less than 8. Right, and when I cross it out, then I do group by by type. So what that means is that if I go back to this example right here. I'm gonna cross out. Every cost that is... I want to keep all of the ones that are less than eight, meaning that I'll cross this one out and that's it, right? And then I'll do group by, so I have one group here, I have one group here, one group here, and then I have one group here. And then what is what is asking is that go ahead group by by type and give me the type and they count the star. So the result is going to be two to one. So I have two rows remaining and then two rows remaining and then one remaining. So just making sure that you understand the difference between Um, their clause where it does row filtering and having clause that actually filters the group by categories that we have. So I'm just gonna double check myself. uh... two, one, three. Video: 1: lec21-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X

children.title: SQL Query for Grouping and Conditions
children.summary: The segment discusses an SQL query involving grouping by type and applying conditions on the maximum cost. The query selects the type and counts the number of dishes where the cost is less than eight, using the GROUP BY clause. An additional condition is introduced to ensure that the maximum cost for each type is less than seven. Examples of dish types and their corresponding counts are provided, illustrating the results of the SQL commands executed.

children.title: SQL Query Analysis: C vs D Outputs
children.summary: The segment discusses the outputs of a SQL command comparing options C and D. Both options have the same columns but differ in how they handle the maximum name selection. The focus is on filtering for gluten-free items and grouping by type. The maximum name for the appetizer category is determined to be 'fries' when grouped correctly, while 'taco' is the maximum for the entree category. The correct output is confirmed as option D.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And this is your slide, which one of these outputs is the output of running this exact SQL command? A new interface doesn't tell me if you're typing or not, so I'll just need to go. I might got fully. Let's take a look. Most of you are saying D. It looks like that D is highlighted as correct. Some of you are saying C. You don't need to change that, but let me see what the issues with C. So the difference between C and D is that C and D both have the same columns, type and max name, and then just the ordering of getting the maximum of the name that seems like was the problematic issue. When you do maximum of the name on appetizer, let me go back here. When you're looking at the appetizer category and you're doing the maximum. On the name... What's happening? Oh, it has to be gluten-free. So when we're filtering by gluten-free, all of these rows are going to be out. So we're only looking at these ones. When we group by the type, then this is going to be one group. This is going to be another group, right? And then we, when you do maximum on the name, maximum on the name here is going to be just taco and maximum between fries and Edamama is going to be fries and this is the correct response that you open. So the difference between C and D is that C and D both have the same columns, type and max name, and then just the ordering of getting the maximum of the name that seems like was the problematic issue. When you do maximum of the name on appetizer, let me go back here. When you're looking at the appetizer category and you're doing the maximum. On the name... What's happening? Oh, it has to be gluten-free. So when we're filtering by gluten-free, all of these rows are going to be out. So we're only looking at these ones. When we group by the type, then this is going to be one group. This is going to be another group, right? And then we, when you do maximum on the name, maximum on the name here is going to be just taco and maximum between fries and Edamama is going to be fries and this is the correct response that you open.

children.title: Introduction to EDA in SQL with IMDB Dataset
children.summary: The segment discusses the use of SQL for exploratory data analysis (EDA), specifically focusing on the IMDB dataset. It highlights the difference between filtering rows using the WHERE clause and filtering groups with the HAVING clause. The speaker mentions that they will demonstrate how to work with large datasets, including selecting rows from the title table in the IMDB database. Additionally, it emphasizes SQL syntax conventions, such as using all caps for keywords and formatting for readability.

children.title: Handling Data with SQL Queries
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the structure of a database table, highlighting key attributes such as 'tconst' as the primary key, 'titleType' for categorizing titles (e.g., movie or TV movie), and other fields like 'primaryTitle', 'originalTitle', 'isAdult', 'startYear', and 'runtimeMinutes'. Emphasis is placed on the importance of managing actual data effectively, referencing previous challenges with regular expressions in SQL functionalities. A SQL query example is provided to select random titles from the database.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So you would see that there is something that's called Tcons that sounds like that that is. A primary key in my table, there is the title type, so if it's movie or TV movie or whatnot, the title, the primary title, original title, adult rated or not, the star, year and whatnot. So all of the information that you want. So interesting things that we want to do generally when we have data is to make sure that we can handle actual data properly with it, right? children.title: Text Processing Techniques with SQL and Regex
children.summary: Three methods for text processing are introduced: using percentage symbols for wildcard matching in SQL, where percentages represent any character any number of times, and underscores for single character matches. An example is provided with 'Star Wars' to demonstrate how to capture variations in titles. Additionally, regular expressions are discussed as a more advanced method for pattern matching, allowing for the extraction of titles containing 'Star Wars' regardless of characters before or after it.

Audio: We're not going to go into the nitty-gritty details of how we can do it, but I'm going to tell you three different ways that we can can do text processing on it. So here I'm building a Boolean in condition and I'm saying that I want to make sure that my primary title is like Like is the keyboard that we're using in order to make sure that we capture everything that matches the same pattern and the pattern that I'm giving is a combination of percentages and also some actual alphabetical letters or any kind of character that you want but percentage is actually going to tell you that it replaces any character and any number of times. So that's what it does. If you want to say, for example, I just want to make sure that I capture only one character. We basically use underscore for just looking for one character. So if I'm interested in different the Star Wars, I would say Star Wars is space and then underscore that I want to see like how many Star Wars exist. The Star Wars is space one, Star Wars is space two or so on and so forth. There is also a regular expression version. that we use a different keyboard for here we use like right here we use similar to when we want to pass uh a regular expression pattern and then we just pass the regular expression pattern that we want so here i'm saying that uh just anything before star wars anything after star wars as long as the star wars is in the title i want to extract it and we also put this in the very class So we can easy regular expression kind of work that we can do with the tables that we have. children.title: Using Regular Expressions in SQL Queries
children.summary: The discussion focuses on using regular expressions (regex) in SQL queries to filter data. A specific example is given where the regex pattern '.* Star Wars.*' is used to select titles from a dataset, resulting in 144 rows that match the criteria. The explanation emphasizes the structure of the regex pattern, detailing how it captures variations of titles related to 'Star Wars'.

children.title: Type Conversion and Column Casting in SQL
children.summary: The process of type conversion in SQL is discussed, focusing on converting data types such as hex or string into integers or floating points for mathematical operations. The CAST function is explained, with an example of converting a runtime value from text to integer. The output of the cast operation renames the column to reflect the conversion, and aliasing is introduced to create more user-friendly column names.

children.title: Type Conversion and Column Casting in SQL
children.summary: Discusses the process of type conversion in SQL, specifically how to convert data types such as hex or string into integers or floating points for mathematical operations. It explains the use of the CAST function to change the type of a column, using the example of converting a runtime value from text to integer. The output of the cast operation renames the column to indicate the conversion, and aliasing can be applied to create more user-friendly column names.

children.title: Using CASE for Conditional Statements in SQL
children.summary: The CASE statement in SQL enables the implementation of conditional logic similar to Python's if statements. It evaluates conditions for a specified column and assigns values based on those conditions. The syntax includes defining a condition followed by the value to assign if the condition is met, with the possibility of adding multiple conditions and an ELSE clause for default assignments. This functionality allows for the creation of a new column that can be included in the output.

children.title: Using CASE for Conditional Statements in SQL
children.summary: The CASE statement in SQL allows for the creation of conditional logic similar to Python's if statements. It evaluates conditions for a specified column and assigns values based on those conditions. The syntax involves specifying a condition followed by a value to assign if the condition is met, with the option to include multiple conditions and an ELSE clause for default assignments. This statement effectively creates a new column that can be selected in the output.

Audio: So this is another EDA item that we want to have. What if we want to run conditions? So this is a little bit complicated, but sometimes it's necessary for you to know, like imagine that you want to actually run if condition, right? So we can do that in Python. What if we can also do it in SQL? So what I can do is I would say that case, um, kind of clauses going to give us that. I would say case. When there is this condition that I'm specifying, then go ahead and assign the same value, assign a certain value. What case does is that it takes one column and then it will try to do different operations on the values in that column, depending on each one of the conditions that you're specifying. So here I'm saying that if you have condition one, do turn the value into this value that I'm specifying. If it doesn't fall into the first category, but it falls into the second category or second condition, then we would do exactly the same thing, and this is the other value that we have. We do everything this way. So as if you can put multiple if conditions up until the end where you would say, else, then assign a value and then you would end a clause by n. children.title: SQL Query for Movie Age Classification
children.summary: A SQL query is demonstrated to classify movies into three categories based on their start year: 'old' for movies before 1950, 'mid-aged' for those from 1950 to 1999, and 'new' for movies from 2000 onwards. The query selects the title type, start year, and includes a new column for movie age, with results ordered randomly and limited to the top 10 rows.

children.title: SQL Query for Movie Age Classification
children.summary: A SQL query is demonstrated to classify movies based on their start year into three categories: 'old' for movies before 1950, 'mid-aged' for those between 1950 and 1999, and 'new' for those from 2000 onwards. The query selects the title type, start year, and a newly created column for movie age, ordering the results randomly and limiting the output to the top 10 rows.

children.title: Introduction to CASE Statements in SQL
children.summary: The lecture introduces CASE statements in SQL, highlighting their similarity to Python's if statements. It outlines the structure of the CASE statement, which evaluates conditions and assigns values based on those evaluations. This functionality allows for the creation of new columns that can be included in the output.

children.title: Introduction to CASE Statements in SQL
children.summary: A new column created in pandas has been discussed, emphasizing its practical application. The lecture then transitions to the topic of conditional statements in SQL using the CASE statement, which functions similarly to Python's if statements. The structure of the CASE statement is outlined: it evaluates conditions and assigns values accordingly, allowing the creation of new columns that can be selected in the output.

children.title: Understanding Joins, Fact Tables, and Star Schema
children.summary: Joins are essential in databases to connect multiple tables for comprehensive data retrieval, with fact tables summarizing key information and dimension tables providing detailed attributes. The Star schema design minimizes data duplication by organizing data into fact and dimension tables, where the fact table contains central numerical data and foreign keys linking to dimension tables. The importance of joining tables is highlighted through examples, such as a product table combined with a store information table, and a cats database illustrating the separation of responsibilities in data organization. The inner join concept in SQL is introduced, where rows from two tables are combined based on matching IDs, retaining only those with corresponding IDs.

children.title: Understanding Joins and Fact Tables in Databases
children.summary: Joins are necessary in databases to connect multiple tables that hold different aspects of data, allowing for comprehensive information retrieval. A fact table serves as a central summary of data, containing key information such as a list of products, while dimension tables provide detailed attributes related to the entries in the fact table. For example, a product table may summarize various products and their associated details, such as drinks and store information, which are stored in separate dimension tables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we're actually doing proper joining of tables. Today, and we're going to see different kinds of syntaxes in order to do join. So the first thing is that why do we need to do a join? Remember that I said a database can have multiple different tables and there is separation of responsibilities between different tables. And because of the way that the the tables and database are structured, we might need to actually connect them in order to have some information all in one place. The way that generally tables and databases are structured is that there's always going to be one fact table. Fact table is going to be a summary of the entire data in the database. So for example, here in my fact table, I have a list of all the products. I call my fact table, the product table, it has some information about drinks, some information about topping, some information about the stores who would sell that a specific product, right? But then the details of the products, the details of the stores or the details of the drinks don't need to sit in that central table. So those details are going to live in dimension tables. Those are different dimensions, the specifying what exactly each one of those attributes in the fact table that I had are corresponding to. Again, notice here that the drink actually now has a bunch of different information. Video: 1: lec21-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X

leaf_chunk.audio: So we're actually doing proper joining of tables. Today, and we're going to see different kinds of syntaxes in order to do join. So the first thing is that why do we need to do a join? Remember that I said a database can have multiple different tables and there is separation of responsibilities between different tables. And because of the way that the the tables and database are structured, we might need to actually connect them in order to have some information all in one place. The way that generally tables and databases are structured is that there's always going to be one fact table. Fact table is going to be a summary of the entire data in the database. So for example, here in my fact table, I have a list of all the products. I call my fact table, the product table, it has some information about drinks, some information about topping, some information about the stores who would sell that a specific product, right? But then the details of the products, the details of the stores or the details of the drinks don't need to sit in that central table. So those details are going to live in dimension tables. Those are different dimensions, the specifying what exactly each one of those attributes in the fact table that I had are corresponding to. Again, notice here that the drink actually now has a bunch of different information.

children.title: Star Schema in Multidimensional Data
children.summary: The Star schema is a database design that minimizes data duplication by organizing data into fact and dimension tables. The fact table contains central numerical data and foreign keys linking to dimension tables, which hold detailed information. For example, the fact table includes drink_id, topping_id, and store_id, while dimension tables provide attributes like name, ice level, and sweetness for drinks, and store details such as store_name and location. Dimension tables generally have more columns but fewer records compared to fact tables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It has a name, it has ice level, it has sweetness and whatnot. One thing that you notice is that this fact table holds the kind of a, holds the foreign key or kind of an ID or key into each one of those dimension tables. So notice that the fact table has drink ID, Topping ID, which is here and the store ID. And then each one of those tables is just going to have those IDs and then more details about those IDs. So this is called the Star schema. And this is kind of the way that we try to make sure that the duplication of the data is as minimal as possible. Video: 1: lec21-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X

children.title: Joining Tables in Database Management
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the importance of joining tables in databases, specifically using a product table and a store information table. It emphasizes the need to combine different sets of data, such as details of drinks with product information. The example provided involves a cats database with two tables: one for cat names and IDs (table S) and another for cat breeds and IDs (table T). This illustrates the concept of separating responsibilities in data organization and sets the stage for learning different types of joins.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And anyone who wants to know what kind of information that we have, they can just take a look at the fact table and just make sense of, OK, what kind of data can I get from this entire database? If I want to know more about the stores, then there is a store information. There should be a table that talks about the stores, and then they would go ahead and take a look at that. So let's assume that I have this product table and I want to actually connect or add the details of the drinks into this table. So I need to make sure that there is a way that I can combine these two sets or these two tables together so that now I have a table that has all the ideas plus the details of the drinks for whatever reason you might want to do that. And this is kind of the way that, or the reason that we need to learn about joining of tables, which is going to be really important. So the data sets that we're going to work with in order to teach you different kinds of joins is kind of cats database that we created. It has two different tables in it and we're going to try to do different types of joins on two data sets or two tables that we have. And these are different cat breeds, and that's my cat dounder. So what we have is table S, table T. So table S has a bunch of names and IDs for different cats that exist. And then table T has IDs, but in addition to that, it also has different breeds for different cats. So this is kind of a separation of responsibilities. One table and breeds one table on the names of them. Video: 1: lec21-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X

children.title: Inner Join Concept in SQL
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the concept of an inner join in SQL, where rows from two tables are combined based on matching IDs. It is explained that when combining tables, only rows with corresponding IDs are retained, while those without matches are excluded. An example is provided with cat breeds, illustrating how the breed of a specific cat can be identified by matching IDs across the tables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So now assume that I want to combine them. I want to know that, okay, what's the breed of the boot's cap? And we would say that, oh, if we combine them, we would notice that that green row corresponds to that other green row because they share the same ID. And I'm going to use IDs in order to find references between the tables. And I would say that, oh, the type is the Persian cat. Okay, so let's see how this can actually be done. children.title: Understanding Inner Join in SQL
children.summary: An inner join combines rows from two tables based on matching primary keys, returning only the rows with corresponding elements in both tables. For example, if one table has an entry for an 'Apricot' cat with no breed reference, it will be omitted if there's no match in the breed table. The inner join retrieves common information, allowing for the selection of specific columns, such as name and breed, while combining data from both tables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You have seen some of these examples before. This is just a review and just telling you the syntax and we have actually nice visuals to show you how it can be done. In your join is the time that I have a bunch of, I have two tables and I have ideas of references or primary keys in two different tables. And I will use those primary keys in order to find corresponding rows. I will only return the rows that have corresponding element in the table S and also corresponding rows in table T. I'll combine them and I will return the value. Meaning that what's happening in this case is that we don't know, for example, the breed for apricot, apricot with the idea of zero does not have a reference in the breed table. And also there is some reference for another cat with the breed of Persian, with the idea of five, which we don't know their name. Right? So there is kind of this information, but the inner join is going to look at the common information between the two tables. Here, the values with ID 1, 2, and 4 have corresponding references. And then we would say that let's go ahead and combine them. When we combine them, we basically borrow all of those columns that each one of those tables have. So s.id is coming from this table, name is coming from this table, and t.id is coming from here, and then breed is going to come from here. If you don't want all the columns, you could really specify that do inner join, but only give me, for example, name and breed. Video: 1: lec21-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X

children.title: Inner Join in SQL with Pandas
children.summary: An inner join combines every row from the first table with its matching entry in the second table, omitting any rows from either table that do not have a match. This behavior is the default for the pd.merge function in Pandas.

children.title: Inner Join in SQL with Pandas
children.summary: An inner join combines every row from the first table with its matching entry in the second table, omitting any rows from either table that do not have a match. This behavior is the default for the pd.merge function in Pandas.

children.title: SQL Joins: Inner, Cross, and Left Outer Joins
children.summary: The segment covers the concept of inner joins in SQL, explaining that it combines rows from two tables based on matching entries, using the 'on' clause to specify the keys, such as 'ID'. It also introduces cross joins, which create a Cartesian product of all combinations of rows from two tables, useful for generating simulation data. The relationship between cross joins and inner joins is discussed, emphasizing that filtering a cross join with conditions results in an inner join. Additionally, left outer joins are introduced, which retain all entries from the left table while replacing unmatched values from the right table with nulls.

children.title: Understanding Inner Join in SQL
children.summary: An inner join combines every row from the first table with its matching entry in the second table, omitting rows without matches. The syntax involves selecting columns from the tables and specifying the inner join operation using the keyword 'inner join'. To find corresponding rows, the 'on' clause is used, indicating which keys from each table should be matched. In this case, the key is 'ID' from both tables.

Audio: You could actually also say say that I won ID because you always want to hold a primary key in your table. This is similar to pandas.merge. So you've seen merge. It essentially does inner join. So the thin packs for it, the way that it works is that we would say, select the star, give me all the columns. You could also specify the columns that you want. And here I'm saying that from what table? So now you're constructing the table that you have. So I'm going to construct the table. I would say that there is table one and there is table two. And the operation that you're going to do on these two tables is that you're going to do inner join. That's the keyword that we'll use. Table one, table two, inner join between them. And in addition to that, how do you do inner join? How do you find corresponding rows with each other? The way that you correspond, you find corresponding rows is that we would say on, this is going to find corresponding rows. children.title: Inner Join and Cross Join in SQL
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of inner joins in SQL, explaining how to select data from two tables (S and T) based on matching IDs. It emphasizes the importance of primary keys for referencing data across tables. The syntax for performing an inner join is introduced, along with the specification of the tables and the condition for matching entries. Additionally, the concept of cross joins is briefly mentioned, highlighting that they allow for joining tables without excluding any rows.

So these are the two tables and then we're doing inner join and then we're saying that on the condition that S dot ID is equal to T dot ID. So that's why primaries are important because it will help us do make reference to different table and be able to. Bring back the data relevant data we have sitting somewhere else into the joint table. So this is Inner Join. We specify the type of join. We specify the tables that we're doing join and I'd be also to specify how you can find matching entries for us. There's a concept of cross-joint. children.title: Understanding Cross Join in SQL
children.summary: A cross join, also known as a Cartesian product, generates every possible combination of rows from two tables. The explanation includes how each row from one table connects with every row from another, resulting in a comprehensive list of combinations. This method can be useful in scenarios such as creating simulation data, although it may not always be practical for all datasets.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I don't want to throw it out because I just don't know the breed. I just want to make sure that I keep everything. So the way that generally the cross-joint also works is that we connect every row in the table T with in table S with every row in table T. So everything, every possible combination is going to be extracted. In this specific case, it really doesn't make sense. That like you don't necessarily want to connect apricot with a bunch of different breeds that you have. But in some cases, it might make sense, especially when you're trying to create simulation data. So here I would connect the first row with all of the rows that I have. I would connect the second row with all of the rows that I have. I would connect the third row and the fourth row and all of these things. And the output is going to be this long list. children.title: Cross Join and Inner Join Explanation
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of cross joins in SQL, explaining how to filter a cross join using a condition to achieve an inner join. It illustrates that when the condition for equality between IDs from two tables is not met, those rows are excluded, resulting in an inner join output. Additionally, it introduces left outer joins, which preserve all entries from the left table, replacing unmatched values from the right table with nulls, exemplified by the case of an apricot without a breed.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's across combination of these two tables that I'm going to combine them together. So, what's happening is that if I use the cross join and I put a condition or a very condition and I would say that create the cross join but filter this cross join on the condition that s that i d is equal to t dot i d. What's going to happen? s dot id should be equal to t dot id. Is this row going to meet that condition? No, right? Because 0 is not equal to 1. 0 is not equal to 2. 0 is not equal to 4. 0 is not equal to 5. So all of these rows are going to be crossed out. Right? And then what's going to remain is what? is going to be all the rows that had corresponding values which effectively means that the output is going to be our inner join. So. kind of two ways of arriving at the same thing but if you want to do inner join just do in your joint don't go around this kind of cycle but just showing you that there is correspondences between them that you can actually filter from cross joint and get the inner join between the tables. There is left outer joint or left joint. Left outer joint says that I want to make sure that all the entries in my left table or the first table that you're specifying in the join are going to be preserved. If a road does not have a corresponding value in the right table, which is the case for for example, for poor apricot here. We would say just just keep the bro put those values equal to null and I would just carry on with just having apricot in my data in my data set but the apricot is not going to have a breed. children.title: Left Outer Join in SQL
children.summary: A left outer join (or left join) retains all rows from the left table and includes only matching rows from the right table, filling in NULL for any missing values. The left table is defined as the one referenced first in the JOIN statement.

children.title: Handling Null Values in Joins
children.summary: When performing joins, if a value from one table (e.g., apricot) does not have a corresponding entry in another table, it will still appear with a null value for its ID and breed information. For matching entries, values will be combined, and it's noted that keeping both IDs (s.id and t.id) is redundant; s.id can be retained as the primary key. In a right join, all rows from the right table are preserved, with additional information from the left table included when available, otherwise filled with nulls. The focus is on understanding how to manage and interpret these null values in the context of the data being analyzed.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what's happening is that apricot doesn't have a corresponding value, it will still remain, but with null value corresponding to the t.id and the breed information. For the cases that we do have matching information, we would match and we would use those values, and then we combine everything that output is going to have all of those columns that we have. So one thing that I want you to note is that you don't necessarily need to hold on to s.id and t.id. Because they are redundant, right? You could just directly say that you're going to keep s.id. Knowing that t.id is also going to be the same, right? Because you did inner join, for example, in the cases that you're doing inner join. And also rename s.id as id, so that now you have one primary key that's named id. Same kind of issue with the right joint. So right joint would say that I want to keep all of these rows in my right table, but I'm going to just find that if there is any additional information from the left table that can be combined. If there is no additional information from the left table that can come to this combination, I'll just fill it with null values, which is going to give us this. All of these are going to be filled with null values. But if the value is the exists, you would just go ahead and combine that. Right, this is the case that you're interested in. All different breeds, but you also want to add the name information, but if you don't have the name, you don't care about it so much, but you just want to know that later on in your sample, how many, for example, Persian cast do you have? So one thing that I want you to note is that you don't necessarily need to hold on to s.id and t.id. Because they are redundant, right? You could just directly say that you're going to keep s.id. Knowing that t.id is also going to be the same, right? Because you did inner join, for example, in the cases that you're doing inner join. And also rename s.id as id, so that now you have one primary key that's named id. Same kind of issue with the right joint. So right joint would say that I want to keep all of these rows in my right table, but I'm going to just find that if there is any additional information from the left table that can be combined. If there is no additional information from the left table that can come to this combination, I'll just fill it with null values, which is going to give us this. All of these are going to be filled with null values. But if the value is the exists, you would just go ahead and combine that. Right, this is the case that you're interested in. All different breeds, but you also want to add the name information, but if you don't have the name, you don't care about it so much, but you just want to know that later on in your sample, how many, for example, Persian cast do you have?

children.title: Right and Full Outer Joins in SQL
children.summary: The discussion covers right and full outer joins in SQL. A right join retains all rows from the right table (T) and matches with the left table, filling in with nulls where there are no matches. The order of columns is determined by the join type, with left table columns appearing first in a left join. Full outer join combines all rows from both tables, filling in nulls for non-matching entries, making it a crucial operation alongside the more commonly used inner join.

First, the columns of the left table is going to come, the second the columns of the right table is going to come, unless you specify a different order. So here because I said everything that's the order that is going to give me is this starts from left to the right, otherwise you just specify the order that you want to work with. If it doesn't have a corresponding left value, put null and then combine null with the values that you have in the right table. This is a full outer joint. children.title: Using Aliasing in SQL Joins
children.summary: Aliasing in SQL joins helps simplify queries by allowing the use of shorter references for table names. For example, instead of repeatedly typing full table names, one can assign 'T' to the title table and 'R' to the rating table. This practice enhances query clarity and conciseness. The segment also discusses performing an inner join based on matching primary keys, specifically using the condition where 't.tcons' is equal to 'r.tcons'. Additionally, a brief explanation of left outer joins is provided, highlighting that they retain all rows from the left table while including only matching rows from the right table, filling in NULL for non-matching entries.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: A lot of times for just making sure that the syntax of doing the joint on different tables is concise. We don't reuse the name of the tables over and over again. We would say that I want to join tables. I want to, for example, here I would say that I want only these two columns, which is good, good practice. Don't throw everything in the final table that you have. But here I would say a one table title and I want table rating. This is from the IMDB database that I have. What I'm doing is I'm doing inner join. But I don't want to keep typing, rating, and title over and over again if I want to select to do different operations on different columns of these tables. Instead, I would say that from this point and on, I'm going to refer to the table title as T and I'm going to refer to table rating as R. This is aliasing, but aliasing in joins are going to be really useful in making the query a little bit more concise. Then I can go ahead and say that okay now do the checking of the rows that t dot or the table t dot tcons is equal to r dot tcons and then those are the cases that the primary keys are the same so there is the corresponding value. Here you go do the the inner joint, that's the output. Otherwise, it would not be a big deal, but you would just need to write, I do. Video: 1: lec21-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X

leaf_chunk.audio: A lot of times for just making sure that the syntax of doing the joint on different tables is concise. We don't reuse the name of the tables over and over again. We would say that I want to join tables. I want to, for example, here I would say that I want only these two columns, which is good, good practice. Don't throw everything in the final table that you have. But here I would say a one table title and I want table rating. This is from the IMDB database that I have. What I'm doing is I'm doing inner join. But I don't want to keep typing, rating, and title over and over again if I want to select to do different operations on different columns of these tables. Instead, I would say that from this point and on, I'm going to refer to the table title as T and I'm going to refer to table rating as R. This is aliasing, but aliasing in joins are going to be really useful in making the query a little bit more concise. Then I can go ahead and say that okay now do the checking of the rows that t dot or the table t dot tcons is equal to r dot tcons and then those are the cases that the primary keys are the same so there is the corresponding value. Here you go do the the inner joint, that's the output. Otherwise, it would not be a big deal, but you would just need to write, I do.

children.title: Right Outer Join in SQL
children.summary: A right outer join retains all rows from the right table and only matching rows from the left table, filling in NULL for any missing values. The right table is defined as the one mentioned second in the JOIN statement. An example SQL query demonstrates this concept, showing how to select data from two tables using a right join.

children.title: Right Outer Join in SQL
children.summary: A right outer join retains all rows from the right table and only matching rows from the left table, filling in NULL for any missing values. The right table is defined as the one mentioned second in the JOIN statement. An example SQL query illustrates this concept, showing how to select data from both tables involved in the join.
