children.title: Common Table Expressions and Right Outer Join
children.summary: Common table expressions (CTEs) are introduced as a complex but useful SQL feature. The segment explains the concept of a right outer join, which retains all rows from the right table and only matching rows from the left table, filling in NULLs for any unmatched values. An example SQL query demonstrates how to implement a right outer join, clarifying that the 'right table' is the one referenced second in the JOIN statement.

children.title: Creating Good Action Movies Table and Actor Analysis
children.summary: A common table expression is utilized to create a 'Good Action Movies' table by performing an inner join between title and rating tables, filtering for action genre movies, including those labeled as drama/action or action/drama. Criteria for good action movies include an average rating greater than seven and at least 5,000 votes to ensure reliability. A query is constructed to identify prolific actors by joining the name table with the principal table, focusing on actors in principal roles categorized as 'actor', and grouping results by actor names. The information retrieved includes movie titles, actor names, number of roles played, and average movie ratings, derived from the respective tables.

children.title: Creating a Table of Good Action Movies
children.summary: A common table expression is used to compile multiple queries efficiently. The focus is on creating a table named 'Good Action Movies' by performing an inner join between the title and rating tables. This process filters for movies categorized under the action genre, allowing the extraction of titles that fit the criteria, including those labeled as drama/action or action/drama.

children.title: Filtering Action Movies and Prolific Actors
children.summary: To identify good action movies, the criteria include an average rating greater than seven and at least 5,000 votes. This ensures the rating is reliable by considering the number of votes. For prolific actors, a query is constructed to join the name table with the principal table, ensuring that only actors in principal roles are included, specifically those categorized as 'actor'. The results will group by actor names to extract a list of prolific actors.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's why we have percent before and percent after. So all action like movies are going to be extracted. And then here we're saying that average rating be greater than seven. So there was a column average rating. We want to make sure that the rating is greater than seven. And we want to make sure that the number of votes is above 5,000. So this is how you would also kind of take a look at the Yelp rating. So you just don't go by the number. You also take a look at the number of votes that gave that average, right? So that's what we're doing. We want to make sure that there are at least 5,000 votes and then the average rating is above 7 that we get. So this is called good action movies right we do exactly the same thing for prolific action prolific actors so we do exactly similar thing we would say that i want to take a look at i want to join the name table and the principal table principal table is a table that He likes all the principal actors and actresses in different movies that we have. So we just want to make sure that... If there is an actor or actress in the movie, they have a principal role. And then we would say that the category of the role should be actor and then we're grouping by their name. So we're extracting just the names of different actors that have primary role in any of those movies and we call them prolific actors. And then what we would do afterwards is to actually run the actual query that we want. Video: 1: lec21-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X

children.title: Information Retrieval from Action Movies and Actors
children.summary: The discussion focuses on extracting information about good action movies and the prolific actors associated with them. Key details include the primary title of the movies, the actor's name, the number of roles played by the actor to gauge their experience, and the average rating of the movies. This information is derived from two tables: one for good action movies and another for prolific actors.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So now we have prolific actors, we also have good action movies. So what I can do is I can say that give me all the information about all of these movies and also the information about the actor that played the primary role in them. So I would take the primary title, I would take the primary name of the... Um. of the of the person the number of roles that they played so this is like the experience of the actor that's coming from from the from the names and principal tables that we have a number also going to round average rating and I'm going to round the average rating of all of these movies and I'm going to call that a rating. So notice that I'm getting all of that information from the two tables that I had and the two tables that I created. The two tables were good action movies and then it was also prolific actors. 7431959
we did with columns on Tuesday).
SELECT primaryTitle, averageRating
FROM Title AS T INNER JOIN Rating AS R
ON T. tconst = R. tconst;

children.title: IMDB Database Queries and Data Filtering
children.summary: The segment discusses querying the IMDB database using SQLite, focusing on retrieving table schemas and executing queries to filter data. It explains the process of building a pipeline to extract specific fields such as primary title, title type, runtime, and start year from the title table. Additionally, it emphasizes the importance of filtering movies based on runtime, ensuring that values are above zero and below 300 minutes.

children.title: IMDB Database Queries and Data Filtering
children.summary: The segment discusses querying the IMDB database using SQLite, highlighting how to retrieve table schemas and execute queries to filter data. It covers the process of building a pipeline to extract specific fields such as primary title, title type, runtime, and start year from the title table. Additionally, it emphasizes filtering movies based on runtime, ensuring values are above zero and below 300 minutes.

children.title: Using SQL Aliases and Filtering Action Movies
children.summary: Aliases in SQL simplify referencing long table names, allowing easier column referencing in queries. A query example demonstrates selecting primaryTitle and averageRating from aliased Title and Rating tables. The segment covers filtering datasets to retrieve top-rated action movies by constructing a SQL query with conditions for genre, minimum votes, and title type. The output includes primary title, main actor's name, number of roles, and average rating, sorted by rating and roles. Additionally, query results are stored in a data frame for exploratory data analysis, integrating DockDB with Pandas for visualization. Trends in action movie ratings over the years are analyzed, calculating median and quartile ratings, and exploring the relationship between runtime and ratings through plotting. The discussion also introduces Common Table Expressions (CTEs) for composing multiple SQL queries.

children.title: Using Aliases in SQL Joins
children.summary: Aliases simplify referencing long table names in SQL queries. For example, in a query that joins the Title and Rating tables, the tables can be aliased as T and R respectively. This allows for easier column referencing, as demonstrated in the SQL statement that selects primaryTitle and averageRating from the aliased tables.

children.title: Filtering and Querying Action Movies in SQL
children.summary: The segment discusses how to filter a dataset to retrieve top-rated action movies by constructing a SQL query. The query includes conditions to select genres related to action, ensure a minimum number of votes (above a thousand), and specify that the title type is a movie. The output includes the primary title, the main actor's name, the number of roles the actor has played, and the average rating of the movies, sorted by rating and the number of roles. Additionally, it hints at the potential for more complex queries.

So this is also what we took a look. What I want is I want the top-rated action movies. So you start with some query and then you will keep adding functionalities to your query so that it does some additional thing that you want. Everything is the same as before except for here I'm also saying that in the ver clause I would say that give me all the genres that are like actions. So I want to get all the action-like movies. And I want to also make sure that the number of votes is above a thousand. And then the type of the title is movie, and so on and so forth. So we're going to take a look. Now you're seeing that I have all different genres that have some action in their name. So... The the common table expression that you saw let me run it so that you can see what the output is going to look like it took a while for it to run but notice that I have primary title I have the primary name of the actor who played the primary role in the movie I have the number of roles that this actor actually played generally in my data set so the number of movies that they played in and then the average rating of this movie so I sorted. Actually, I ordered by rating and then I ordered also by the number of rows that the actor played. So these are like the highest values that I get. children.title: Data Frame Visualization and SQL Query Results
children.summary: The speaker discusses storing query results in a data frame called action_movie_df, which includes values such as duration and start year for exploratory data analysis (EDA). They emphasize the integration of DockDB with Pandas for visualization and modeling. The speaker mentions visualizing the relationship between the year and ratings using this data frame.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Um. I can go ahead and store this value in the data frame. So this is also the same set of values that I have. Now I have added also the duration in time. I also added the start year so I can do a little bit more EDA on this data frame. So what I'm going to do next is that because now that result of the query was in a data frame, I could actually go ahead and plot it. That's why DockDB is really interesting because DockDB really connects well with with tendus. So what I did before here was that I said run the squery is store the result in a data frame that's called action underscore movie underscore df. That is a data frame. Once I have the data frame it's literally going back to the pandas lectures you can do visualization you can do modeling and everything that you want. children.title: Analyzing Action Movie Ratings Over Time
children.summary: The discussion focuses on analyzing trends in action movie ratings over the years, including calculating the median, first quartile, and third quartile ratings for each year. It emphasizes the use of SQL queries to summarize large datasets, which can then be visualized in a data frame. Additionally, the segment explores the relationship between movie runtime and ratings by plotting duration against ratings, suggesting an approach towards regression analysis.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I want to take a look at okay over all of those action movies over all of those years. How did the rating change over time? Right, so you can notice the trend. And what we can actually do... is I can actually go ahead and say that I want to do further processing and I want you to also show me that for every year what is the median rating and what is the first quartile and the third quartile of it because it was a lot of overplodding right here. So in that case, if I go ahead and visualize it, you will see the trend. It's like fancy stuff you can get a really big data set. You can do, we can run SQL queries. You can get a summary. Your summary is going to be small enough that you can actually port it into a data frame and then you can actually visualize the stuff. So everything all together. What if I go ahead and say that, can I understand whether the runtime of action movies influence the rating of the movie? So what I'm going to do is I'm going to actually plot on the X-axis the duration of the movie with respect to the rating to see if I can see any trend. It sounds like that I'm going into the direction of actually doing some regression analysis. So let's take a look. Again, in the data frame, I have all of these features, So this was the data frame that's result of my common expression table that I actually created. But now I'm actually plotting time with respect to the rating. Video: 1: lec21-demo.i ... (2) - JupyterLab X

7431959
WITH
WITH
good_action_movies AS (
table_name1 AS (
SELECT *
SELECT ...
FROM Title T JOIN Rating R ON T.tconst = R.tconst
WHERE genres LIKE '%Action%' AND averageRating > 7 AND numVotes > 5000
),
).
table_name2 AS (
prolific_actors AS (
SELECT ...
SELECT N.nconst, primaryName, COUNT(*) as numRoles
FROM Name N JOIN Principal P ON N.nconst = P.nconst
WHERE category = 'actor'
SELECT ...
GROUP BY N.nconst, primaryName
FROM
)
SELECT primaryTitle, primaryName, numRoles, ROUND(averageRating) AS rating
table_name1,
FROM good_action_movies m, prolific_actors a, principal p
table_name2, ...
WHERE p.tconst = m.tconst AND p.nconst = a.nconst
ORDER BY rating DESC, numRoles DESC
LIMIT 10

children.title: Common Table Expressions in SQL
children.summary: Common Table Expressions (CTEs) allow for the composition of multiple SQL queries. An example demonstrates how to create a CTE named 'good_action_movies' that selects action movies with an average rating above 7 and over 5000 votes. Another CTE, 'prolific_actors', counts the number of roles for actors. The final query combines these CTEs to select the primary title, actor name, number of roles, and rounded average rating, ordering the results by rating and number of roles.

children.title: Feature Engineering and Model Evaluation in SQL
children.summary: The segment discusses feature engineering techniques, including the creation of heat maps to visualize movie ratings based on duration. It also explores the relationship between time and median ratings, suggesting a trend analysis. Additionally, a linear regression model is fitted to assess trends. The importance of SQL for running large queries on datasets is emphasized, along with its integration with pandas for data manipulation and visualization. The session concludes with a reminder about upcoming lab and homework assignments related to SQL, and the introduction of a new topic in the following week.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: More feature engineering so it needs to have some transformation because it's not actually showing a linear trend but if I was curious and I wanted to actually create a heat map I could actually look at the heat map of all of these values of how many How many movies did we have that had that were like 100 minutes and then the rating average rating was 5.9 so you can actually find out those values with this heat map. The next thing that I would do is quickly showing you the trend kind of similar thing, that if I'm looking at the time and I'm looking at the median rating, what's that trend? So as the time increases, is there any influence on the median rating or not? And the last thing is that I am doing, I am filling fancy and I will fit a linear regression to the plot that we had before so that we can kind of see if there is any trend or not. So I walk you through the entire process. I gave you a big data and I told you that there is a new technology that you should use and be comfortable to use SQL in order to actually run big queries on the datasets. Once you run all of those big queries, you can do also some of the operations as part of those queries. We spend a lecture and half actually seeing that. Then we saw that it actually connects really well with pandas. We ported everything into data frame. Then we went ahead and actually did a bunch of different visualization and modeling. That's kind of a wrap up. The next thing that after this, you would need to do is just evaluating your model, but you're all familiar with it. Okay, that was it today. We are going to come back next week with a new topic. As I promised every week, there is going to be a new module. So this was a wrap up for SQL. You're going to do one lab and one homework on it. children.title: Common Table Expressions in SQL
children.summary: Common Table Expressions (CTEs) allow for composing multiple queries in SQL. An example provided includes a CTE named 'good_action_movies' that selects action movies with an average rating greater than 7 and more than 5000 votes. Another CTE, 'prolific_actors', counts the number of roles for actors. The final query combines these CTEs to select movie titles, actor names, number of roles, and average ratings, ordering the results by rating and number of roles.
